narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.30—below the typical 3.21 average—along with an LSAT score of 28, which is notably lower than the average of around 37, and early grades placing you in the second decile of your Year 1 class compared to the average fifth decile. Your family's middle-income bracket and plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students, also factor into the picture. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past law students, predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading directly to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed. This high failure probability means the model saw your overall academic indicators as strong signals of risk, even though many applicants share similar backgrounds like yours. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 decile, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile—landing in the first percentile compared to the average fifth—had the strongest pull toward expecting a bar failure, as it suggests challenges persisting through your later undergraduate years. Your Year 1 decile in the second spot reinforced this concern, deviating from the stronger mid-range performance seen in typical successful students. Even your LSAT score, while not the top driver, further tipped the scales negatively by falling well short of what the model associates with bar passers. In contrast to average applicants who pass the bar, your metrics across these key academic areas are consistently lower, creating a pattern the model couldn't overlook. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as an Asian male navigating these challenges. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could significantly shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to approval next cycle. Many students in similar positions have turned things around with targeted improvements, and your full-time commitment positions you well for that effort.
